content:
# Leviticus Chapter 7 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 60v–61r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_007_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_007_page_002.jpg`; **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 91–92 (LXX) | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_007_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_007_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

*[Note on structure: Leviticus 7 concludes the sacrificial manual begun in Lev 1. Vv.1-10 complete the guilt offering (asham) law. Vv.11-21 develop the peace offering (zevach ha-shelamim) with its three sub-forms: todah (thanksgiving), neder (vow), and nedavah (freewill). Vv.22-27 state the absolute prohibition of fat and blood for all Israel. Vv.28-34 define the wave offering (tenufah) and contribution portion (terumah) procedure. Vv.35-38 provide the summary colophon for Lev 1-7. MT and English versification are identical throughout this chapter.]*

---

**1** And this is the torah [instruction] of the guilt offering [zot torat ha-asham] — it is most holy [qodesh qodashim hu]:

**2** In the place where they slaughter the burnt offering [bimqom asher yishchatu et-ha-olah] they shall slaughter the guilt offering [yishchatu et-ha-asham]; and its blood [ve-et-damo] he shall dash [yizroq] against the altar on all sides [al-ha-mizbeach saviv].

**3** And all its fat [ve-et kol-chelbo] he shall offer from it [yaqriv mimmennu]: the fat tail [ha-alyah] and the fat that covers the entrails [ha-chelev ha-mechasse et-ha-qerev],

**4** and the two kidneys [ve-et shtey ha-kelayot] and the fat that is on them [ve-et-ha-chelev asher alehem] which is by the flanks [asher al-ha-kesalim], and the lobe on the liver [ve-et ha-yoteret al-ha-kaved] — upon the kidneys he shall remove it [al-ha-kelayot yesirenah].

**5** And the priest shall burn them [ve-hiqtir otam ha-kohen] on the altar as a fire offering [isheh] to the LORD — it is a guilt offering [asham hu].

**6** Every male among the priests [kol-zakhar ba-kohanim] shall eat it [yokhalenah]; in a holy place [bimaqom qadosh] it shall be eaten [ye'akhel] — it is most holy [qodesh qodashim hu].

**7** Like the sin offering [ka-chattat], so is the guilt offering [ka-asham]; one law [torah achat] is for them — the priest who makes atonement with it [ha-kohen asher yekapper bo], his it shall be [lo yihyeh].

**8** And the priest who offers [ha-makriv] any man's burnt offering [et-olat ish] — the skin of the burnt offering [or ha-olah] that he has offered [asher hiqriv], it shall be his [lo tihyeh la-kohen].

**9** And every grain offering [ve-khol-minchah] baked in the oven [asher te'afeh ba-tannur] and every one made on a pan [asher na'asah al-machabat] or on a griddle [ve-al-machashat] — to the priest who offers it [la-kohen ha-maqrivah] it shall belong [lo tihyeh].

**10** And every grain offering mixed with oil [ve-khol-minchah velulab-shemen] or dry [u-vevah] — to all the sons of Aaron it shall belong [le-khol-bney aharon tihyeh], one as well as another [ish ke-achiv].

---

**11** "And this is the torah [instruction] of the sacrifice of peace offerings [zot torat zevach ha-shelamim] that one shall offer [asher yaqriv] to the LORD:

**12** If he offers it for a thanksgiving [al todah] he shall offer along with the sacrifice of thanksgiving [al-zevach ha-todah] unleavened loaves [challot matsot] mixed with oil [belulot ba-shemen], and unleavened wafers [rekikei matsot] spread with oil [meshuchim ba-shemen], and well-soaked loaves [challot solet murbekheth] of fine flour mixed with oil [belulot ba-shemen].

**13** With loaves of leavened bread [al-challot lechem chametz] he shall bring his offering [yaqriv qorbano] alongside the sacrifice of his peace offering for thanksgiving [al-zevach todah shelamav].

**14** And from it he shall offer one from each offering [ve-hiqriv mimmennu echad mi-kol-qorban] as a contribution [terumah] to the LORD — to the priest who dashes the blood of the peace offerings [la-kohen ha-zoreh et-dam ha-shelamim], his it shall be [lo yihyeh].

**15** And the flesh of the sacrifice of his thanksgiving peace offering [u-vesar zevach todat shelamav] shall be eaten [ye'akhel] on the day of his offering [be-yom qorbano] — he shall not leave any of it [lo-yaniach mimmennu] until morning [ad-boqer].

**16** But if the sacrifice of his offering is a vow [neder] or a freewill offering [nedavah], on the day he offers his sacrifice [be-yom haqribo et-zivcho] it shall be eaten [ye'akhel], and on the next day [u-mi-mocharat] what remains of it [ha-notar mimmennu] shall also be eaten [ye'akhel].

**17** And what remains of the flesh of the sacrifice [ve-ha-notar mi-besar ha-zevach] on the third day [ba-yom ha-shelishi] shall be burned with fire [ba-esh yissaref].

**18** And if any of the flesh of the sacrifice of his peace offering [mi-besar zevach shelamav] is ever eaten on the third day [be-yom ha-shelishi he'akhol ye'akhel], it shall not be accepted [lo yeratseb] — he who offers it [ha-maqrib oto] shall not have it reckoned to him [lo yechashev lo]; it shall be a piggul [rejected thing / foul thing] [piggul yihyeh], and the life that eats of it [ve-ha-nefesh ha-okhelet mimmennu] shall bear her iniquity [avonah tissa].

**19** And the flesh that touches any unclean thing [u-ha-basar asher-yigga be-khol-tame] shall not be eaten [lo ye'akhel]; it shall be burned with fire [ba-esh yissaref]; and as for the flesh [ve-ha-basar], every clean person [kol-tahor] may eat the flesh [yokhal basar].

**20** But the life [nefesh] who eats flesh from the sacrifice of peace offerings [asher tokhal basar mi-zevach ha-shelamim] that belongs to the LORD [asher la-YHWH] while his uncleanness is upon him [ve-tum'ato alav] — that life shall be cut off from her peoples [ve-nikhretah ha-nefesh ha-hi mi-ammeha].

**21** And a life [nefesh] who touches any unclean thing [asher tigga be-khol-tame] — in the uncleanness of a human [be-tum'at adam] or in an unclean animal [o be-vehemah temea] or in any unclean detestable thing [o be-khol shequts tame] — and eats [ve-akhal] from the flesh of the sacrifice of peace offerings [mi-besar zevach ha-shelamim] that belongs to the LORD [asher la-YHWH], that life shall be cut off from her peoples [ve-nikhretah ha-nefesh ha-hi mi-ammeha].'"

---

**22** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**23** "Speak to the sons of Israel [daber el-bney yisrael], saying: Any fat [kol-chelev] of ox [shor] or sheep [kesev] or goat [ez] you shall not eat [lo tokheilu].

**24** And the fat of an animal that died [ve-chelev nevela] and the fat of what was torn [ve-chelev terefa] may be used for any purpose [ye'aseh le-khol-melacha], but you shall certainly not eat it [ve-akhol lo tokhelu].

**25** For anyone who eats the fat [ki kol-okhel chelev] of the animal from which a fire offering [min-ha-behemah asher yaqriv mimmenah] is offered to the LORD — the life who eats [ve-nikhretah ha-nefesh ha-okhelet] shall be cut off from her peoples [mi-ammeha].

**26** And you shall not eat any blood [ve-khol-dam lo tokheilu] in any of your dwelling places [be-khol moshevoteikhem], whether of bird [la-of] or of animal [la-vehemah].

**27** Any life who eats any blood [kol-nefesh asher-tokhal kol-dam] — that life shall be cut off from her peoples [ve-nikhretah ha-nefesh ha-hi mi-ammeha].'"

---

**28** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**29** "Speak to the sons of Israel, saying: He who offers [ha-maqrib] the sacrifice of his peace offerings [et-zevach shelamav] to the LORD — he shall bring [yavi] his offering to the LORD [et-qorbano la-YHWH] from the sacrifice of his peace offerings [mi-zevach shelamav].

**30** His own hands [yadav] shall bring the fire offerings [yevi'u et-ishi] of the LORD — the fat [ha-chelev] upon the breast [al-he-chazeh] he shall bring [yevi'enah], and the breast [ve-et-ha-chazeh] to wave as a wave offering [le-hanif tenufah] before the LORD [lifney ha-YHWH].

**31** And the priest shall burn the fat [ve-hiqtir ha-kohen et-ha-chelev] on the altar, but the breast [ve-haya ha-chazeh] shall belong to Aaron [le-aharon] and to his sons [u-le-vanav].

**32** And the right thigh [ve-et shok ha-yamin] you shall give as a contribution [titenu terumah] to the priest from the sacrifices of your peace offerings [mi-zivchey shelameykem].

**33** He among the sons of Aaron [ha-maqriv mi-bney aharon] who offers the blood of the peace offerings and the fat [et-dam ha-shelamim ve-et-ha-chelev] — the right thigh [ha-shok ha-yamin] shall be his as a portion [lo yihyeh le-manah].

**34** For the breast of the wave offering [ki et-chazeh ha-tenufah] and the thigh of the contribution [ve-et shok ha-terumah] I have taken [laqachti] from the sons of Israel [me-et bney yisrael] from the sacrifices of their peace offerings [mi-zivchey shelameyhem] and I have given them [va-etten otam] to Aaron the priest [le-aharon ha-kohen] and to his sons [u-le-vanav] as a perpetual due [le-chuqat olam] from the sons of Israel [me-et bney yisrael].'"

---

**35** This is the anointed portion [zot mishchat aharon] of Aaron and the anointed portion of his sons [u-mishchat banav] from the fire offerings of the LORD [me-ishi ha-YHWH], on the day he presented them [be-yom haqriv otam] to minister as priests to the LORD [le-khahane la-YHWH].

**36** Which the LORD commanded [asher tsivvah ha-YHWH] to give them [latet lahem] on the day he anointed them [be-yom mosho otam] from the sons of Israel [me-et bney yisrael] — a perpetual statute throughout their generations [chuqat olam le-dorotehem].

**37** This is the torah [instruction] of the burnt offering [ha-olah], the grain offering [ha-minchah], the sin offering [ha-chattat], the guilt offering [ha-asham], the ordination offering [ha-millu'im], and the sacrifice of peace offerings [zevach ha-shelamim],

**38** which the LORD commanded Moses [asher tsivvah ha-YHWH et-moshe] on Mount Sinai [be-har sinai] on the day he commanded the sons of Israel [be-yom tsavoto et-bney yisrael] to offer their offerings to the LORD [le-haqriv et-qorbanehem la-YHWH] in the wilderness of Sinai [be-midbar sinai].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Leningrad Codex B19A, Folios 60v–61r)

1. **v.1 — Zot torat ha-asham / "This is the torah of the guilt offering":** The guilt offering (אָשָׁם, asham) law is already introduced at Lev 5:14ff.; here in 7:1-10 it receives its procedural supplement covering priestly portions. The opening formula זֹאת תּוֹרַת הָאָשָׁם (zot torat ha-asham) is parallel to the burnt offering formula (Lev 6:9 [MT]), grain offering formula (Lev 6:14 [MT]), and sin offering formula (Lev 6:25 [MT]); all are gathered into the summary colophon at vv.37-38. The declaration "it is most holy" (קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים הוּא, qodesh qodashim hu) at v.1 immediately categorizes the guilt offering alongside the sin offering in the most-holy class — meaning only male priests may eat of it, only in the court of the tent of meeting. The LXX renders Αὕτη ὁ νόμος τοῦ τῆς πλημμελείας (Hautē ho nomos tou tēs plēmmelias, "This is the law of the trespass/guilt offering"), using πλημμέλεια (plēmmelia, "error/trespass") for asham, a rendering that emphasizes the inadvertent or culpable-error dimension of the asham as distinct from chattat.

2. **v.2 — Bimqom asher yishchatu et-ha-olah / "in the place where they slaughter the burnt offering":** The guilt offering shares the slaughter location of the burnt offering — the north side of the altar (Lev 1:11). This spatial alignment of the asham with the olah (rather than the chattat, which had its own location for the high-priestly and communal versions) is a deliberate architectural feature: the asham is a "reparation offering" most closely resembling the olah in its procedural profile. The blood dashing (זָרַק, zaraq, "to throw/dash") is also shared — the standard peripheral-blood application for most offerings other than the complex atonement smearing of the chattat. The LXX uses ἐναντίον κυρίου (enantion kyriou, "before the Lord") to render al-ha-mizbeach saviv (around the altar), a paraphrase that focuses the gesture's orientation rather than its spatial description.

3. **vv.3-5 — The fat portions of the asham:** The fat-burning prescription for the guilt offering matches that of the peace offering (Lev 3:3-4, 9-10, 14-15): (1) the alyah (fat tail — for sheep/lambs; here applied to the asham animal which is a ram by Lev 5:15); (2) chelev ha-mechasse et-ha-qerev (fat covering the entrails); (3) the two kidneys with their surrounding fat; (4) the yoteret al-ha-kaved (lobe/appendage of the liver). This standardized list of internal fat portions is the shared "altar portion" across multiple offering types. The fat tail (אַלְיָה, alyah) is specifically the broad, fat-laden tail of the fat-tailed sheep (Ovis laticaudata) common in the ancient Near East and still widespread in the Levant — a distinctive feature of the region's sheep breeds. The LXX renders the anatomical terms precisely in Greek: οἱ δύο νεφροί (hoi duo nephroi, "the two kidneys"), ὁ λοβὸς ὁ ἐπὶ τοῦ ἥπατος (ho lobos ho epi tou hēpatos, "the lobe upon the liver").

4. **v.7 — Torah achat / "one law for them":** The principle that the chattat (sin offering) and asham (guilt offering) share "one law" (תּוֹרָה אַחַת, torah achat) concerning priestly portions is a significant leveling statement. Despite their functional differences — the chattat addresses inadvertent violations of prohibitions, the asham covers trespass against sancta and certain sworn/deception offenses — both yield the same priestly entitlement: the officiating priest receives the meat. This unity is reinforced by the shared "most holy" status (v.1, 6). The LXX renders νόμος εἷς ἔσται αὐτοῖς (nomos heis estai autois, "one law shall be for them"), a precise rendering of the Hebrew.

5. **vv.8-10 — Priestly portions from the olah, minchah:** These verses specify which portions of the burnt offering and grain offering belong to the officiating priest — not the shared Aaron-and-sons division but the individual priest's entitlement: (a) the skin of the burnt offering (or ha-olah) to the priest who offers it; (b) every grain offering baked in a tannur (oven), or made on a machabat (flat pan) or machashat (griddle) — to the offering priest specifically; (c) grain offerings mixed with oil or dry — to all the sons of Aaron equally (ish ke-achiv, "man as his brother"). The distinction between v.9 (individual priest) and v.10 (all priestly sons equally) is significant: it reflects a division between preparations that require individual priestly labor (baking/frying) and those that are simply mixed/presented (which are communal priestly property). The LXX preserves this distinction with its rendering of ish kata ton adelphon autou (ish ke-achiv) as πᾶσι τοῖς υἱοῖς Ααρων (pasi tois hyiois Aarōn, "to all the sons of Aaron").

6. **v.12 — Zevach todah / "sacrifice of thanksgiving" — the todah:** The todah (תּוֹדָה) is the highest of the three peace offering sub-forms and introduces Lev 7's central teaching. It is brought specifically "for thanksgiving" (al todah — the preposition al here indicating purpose/occasion). Its distinctive feature is the extraordinary bread-accompaniment: four types of bread — (1) challot matsot belulot ba-shemen (unleavened loaves mixed with oil); (2) rekikei matsot meshuchim ba-shemen (unleavened wafers spread/anointed with oil); (3) challot solet murbekheth belulot ba-shemen (loaves of fine flour that are murbekheth — "thoroughly soaked/softened" — mixed with oil); and (4) challot lechem chametz (loaves of leavened bread). The third type (murbekheth) recalls the high priest's daily grain offering preparation (Lev 6:21 [MT 6:14]). The fourth type is remarkable: leavened bread (chametz) is permitted in this context, a notable exception to the general prohibition of chametz from fire offerings (Lev 2:11) — because the leavened loaves are not burned on the altar but eaten by the worshipper and priests. Rabbinic tradition (Lev. Rabbah, Midrash Tehillim on Ps. 56) holds that in the messianic age all offerings will cease except the todah, because the todah's occasion (deliverance from danger: sea, wilderness, captivity, illness — as developed in Ps. 107) will persist as long as humans face mortal danger. The LXX renders τοδα as αἴνεσις (ainesis, "praise") or θυσίαν αἰνέσεως (thysian aineseōs, "sacrifice of praise"), understanding the todah's root (y-d-h, "to acknowledge, to praise, to give thanks") in its doxological dimension.

7. **v.15 — Day restriction for todah:** The todah's bread must be eaten on the day of offering with nothing left until morning. This one-day eating window (shorter than the two-day window for vow/freewill offerings in v.16) likely reflects the todah's perishability given its large bread requirement and the liturgical urgency of communal celebration of deliverance. The stricter time-limit also prevents the temptation to keep leftover holy food beyond its sanctioned window — any delay risks transformation into piggul (v.18). The phrase לֹא-יַנִּיחַ מִמֶּנּוּ עַד-בֹּקֶר (lo-yaniach mimmennu ad-boqer, "he shall not leave any of it until morning") uses the same language as the Passover lamb restriction (Ex 12:10; Num 9:12), linking the todah's urgency to that foundational deliverance meal.

8. **v.18 — Piggul / "rejected/foul thing":** פִּגּוּל (piggul) is one of the most technical terms in Levitical sacrifice law. A sacrifice becomes piggul when its officiating priest intends during the offering procedure to eat of it beyond its lawful time. The word itself carries connotations of stench and revulsion (cf. Isa 65:4 where piggul describes food rendered offensive). The legal consequence is severe: the offering is "not accepted" (lo yeratseb, "it shall not find favor / it shall not be accepted"), it is "not reckoned" (lo yechashev, "it shall not be credited/counted") to the offerer, and the one who eats of it bears his iniquity (avonah). The specific mechanism — a wrongful mental intention during the ritual procedure contaminating the whole offering retroactively — is a distinctive feature of the priestly system: the priest's intention is constitutive of the offering's validity. Mishnah Zevachim 2:2-4 develops an extensive analysis of piggul: the defiling thought must occur during one of the four prescribed actions (slaughter, receiving the blood, conveying the blood, dashing the blood) and must specifically concern eating the flesh outside its time or place. The LXX renders piggul as μίασμα (miasma, "pollution/defilement"), interpreting the term in terms of its result (defilement) rather than its mechanism (wrongful intent).

9. **vv.22-27 — Fat and blood prohibitions as universal Israel law:** The fat and blood prohibitions that governed the priestly altar portions in Lev 3 (where the fat was burned as the LORD's portion) are here extended explicitly to kol bney yisrael (all the sons of Israel) in any of their dwelling places (be-khol moshevoteikhem). This universalization is significant: it is not merely a ritual restriction for sanctuary contexts but a dietary law governing every Israelite household everywhere. Three categories of fat prohibition are specified: (1) chelev of clean animals whose species can produce altar offerings (shor, kesev, ez — ox, sheep, goat); (2) the fat of nevela (animal that died naturally); (3) the fat of terefa (animal that was torn/killed by predators). The nevela/terefa fat "may be used for any purpose" (le-khol melacha, lit. "for any work/use") — i.e., it has non-food utility (lamps, leather treatment, etc.) but cannot be eaten. The blood prohibition is absolute: "any blood" (kol-dam), of birds or animals, in any dwelling place. The karet penalty (ve-nikhretah, "shall be cut off") applies to both fat-eating (v.25) and blood-eating (v.27). Karet (כָּרֵת) is divine excision — a penalty executed not by human courts but by God himself, involving premature death and/or loss of share in the world to come in rabbinic understanding. The LXX renders ve-nikhretah ha-nefesh as ἐξολεθρευθήσεται ἡ ψυχή (exolethreuthēsetai hē psychē, "the soul shall be utterly destroyed"), using a stronger Greek verb (exolothreuo, "to destroy utterly") than the simple "cut off" of the Hebrew — the LXX intensifies the severity.

10. **vv.30-31 — Tenufah / "wave offering" — the waving gesture:** The tenufah (תְּנוּפָה) is the distinctive gesture for presenting the breast and the thigh of the peace offering before the LORD. The verb from which it derives (נוּף, nuf, "to wave/move back and forth") describes a formal horizontal waving motion — traditionally understood as moving the offering forward and backward before the altar, toward the LORD and back toward the offerer. The breast (חָזֶה, chazeh) is the wave portion; the right thigh (שׁוֹק הַיָּמִין, shok ha-yamin) is the contribution portion (terumah, a lifting-presentation rather than a waving). These two priestly portions — breast to all Aaron's sons, right thigh to the officiating priest — are described in vv.31-34 as permanently established priestly rights (le-chuqat olam). The phrase יָדָיו תְּבִיאֶינָה (yadav tevi'einah, "his own hands shall bring it") in v.30 emphasizes that the offerer himself physically participates in presenting the wave portion — it is not merely handed over to the priest at the sanctuary entrance but brought by the offerer's own hands to be waved before the LORD, a gesture of personal dedication and relinquishment.

11. **vv.35-36 — Mishchat aharon / "the anointed portion of Aaron":** The word מִשְׁחַת (mishchat) at v.35 is unusual — it appears to derive from the root m-sh-ch (to anoint) and functions here as a noun meaning "anointed portion" or "anointing-right." It designates the established share that Aaron and his sons receive from the fire offerings as the right attached to their anointed office. The phrase "on the day he presented them to minister as priests to the LORD" (be-yom haqriv otam le-khahane la-YHWH) anchors these priestly rights in the inauguration of the Aaronic priesthood itself — the ordination ceremony (millu'im) of Lev 8. The priestly portions are thus not perquisites granted ad hoc but constitutive rights established at the founding moment of the priesthood. The LXX renders mishchat as χρίσμα (chrisma, "anointing/anointed gift"), directly reflecting the Hebrew root, and adds ἐν ᾗ ἡμέρᾳ προσήγαγεν αὐτοὺς (en hē hēmera prosēgagen autous, "on the day he brought/presented them near"), preserving the inauguration-anchoring of the rights.

12. **vv.37-38 — Summary colophon:** The closing formula is the most comprehensive in the priestly sacrificial manual. It names six offering types: olah (burnt offering), minchah (grain offering), chattat (sin offering), asham (guilt offering), millu'im (ordination offering), and zevach ha-shelamim (sacrifice of peace offerings). Notably this list includes the millu'im (ordination offerings) which are not formally described until Lev 8 — the colophon already anticipates them, framing Lev 6-7 as the procedural appendix that also covers the upcoming ordination ceremony. The location "Mount Sinai" (har sinai) grounds the entire sacrificial system in the Sinai revelation — not Canaan, not tabernacle-practice, but the original divine disclosure at Sinai. The phrase "in the wilderness of Sinai" (be-midbar sinai) further underlines the wilderness setting: these laws were given before Israel ever entered the land, establishing their character as pre-land, perpetual divine commands rather than merely agrarian or local customs. The LXX preserves the colophon structure and renders be-midbar sinai as ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ Σινα (en tē erēmō Sina), with Σινα (Sina) as the standard LXX transliteration of Sinai.

### LXX (Codex Vaticanus) Observations

The Vaticanus text (pa_0091–0092) aligns closely with the MT through the chapter. Notable divergences:

- **v.3 — The fat tail:** Vaticanus includes ἡ ὀσφύς (hē osphys, "the loin/back") where MT has alyah (fat tail) — possibly a rendering based on a different understanding of the anatomy or a Hebrew variant. The Septuagint translator may not have known the fat-tailed sheep directly and rendered alyah by anatomical proximity.

- **v.18 — Piggul:** The LXX μίασμα (miasma, "defilement") for piggul is an interpretive gloss — the LXX translator has rendered the technical term by its consequence (cultic defilement) rather than attempting a lexical equivalent. This is consistent with LXX's general tendency to moralize or clarify technical priestly vocabulary for a diaspora audience unfamiliar with sacrificial minutiae.

- **v.25 — Fat prohibition:** Vaticanus reads the prohibition specifically limited to the animals listed in v.23 (ox, sheep, goat) — the LXX here is more explicit than the MT in confining the prohibition to altar-eligible species, whereas the MT's wording is broader.

- **vv.35-36 — Mishchat:** The LXX χρίσμα closely mirrors the Hebrew root but functions in Greek as a noun for "anointed gift/right," which is precisely the nuance needed. This is one of the rare cases where a Hebrew technical term finds a near-exact Greek functional equivalent.
